11: I admit, I began as a skeptic. Plausibility, I've found, is often inversely proportional to one's own
12: knowledge of a subject. Early models, for all their fluency, felt like they were operating with a
13: kind of impostor syndrome, optimized for credibility over correctness. But then came the
14: inflection point, a step-change brought about by a new class of "reasoning" models. Suddenly,
15: we weren't just conversing with a statistical machine that predicted the next word in a sequence;
16: we were getting a peek into a nascent form of cognition.
17: The first time I experimented with one of the new agentic coding tools, I felt that familiar spark of
18: magic. I tasked it with a personal project I'd never found the time for: migrating a charity website

24: Of course, it wasn't perfect. It made mistakes. It got stuck. It required my supervision and,
25: crucially, my judgment to steer it back on course. The experience drove home a lesson I've
26: learned the hard way over a long career: you cannot afford to trust blindly. Yet, the process was
27: fascinating. Peeking into its "chain of thought" was like watching a mind at work--messy,
28: non-linear, full of starts, stops, and self-corrections, not unlike our own human reasoning. It
29: wasn't a straight line; it was a random walk toward a solution. Here was the kernel of something
30: new: not just an intelligence that could generate content, but one that could generate a plan.
31: This is the promise of agentic frameworks. It's the difference between a static subway map and
32: a dynamic GPS that reroutes you in real-time. A classic rules-based automaton follows a fixed
33: path; when it encounters an unexpected obstacle, it breaks. An AI agent, powered by a
34: reasoning model, has the potential to observe, adapt, and find another way. It possesses a form
